tldr: MessageBird is a multi-channel platform (SMS, voice, WhatsApp, email), while Postmark is email-only and transactional-focused. Migrating only makes sense for the email portion of your stack; SMS, WhatsApp, and voice need a separate provider.
why_switch: Email deliverability and analytics as primary concern
Better template tooling for receipts and notifications
Reduce vendor surface for the email channel
what_to_export: Verified sending domains and DKIM records
Webhook configurations for delivery and bounce events
export_method: Pull email sending domains, DKIM settings, and templates from your MessageBird workspace using the dashboard export or REST API. Export suppression and bounce lists as CSV. Document any webhook subscriptions that point to your application so you can recreate them on Postmark. If you use MessageBird for non-email channels (SMS, WhatsApp, voice), keep that integration in place — only migrate the email surface. Capture historical metrics for analytics continuity. Keep MessageBird sending active until Postmark DNS, warm-up, and deliverability checks are clean.
import_method: In Postmark, create a server and add your sending domain, then publish the DKIM and Return-Path DNS records Postmark provides. Recreate templates in the Postmark template editor with the same merge variables your application uses. Import the suppression list via the Postmark API. Wire up webhooks for delivery, bounce, and complaint events to your existing handler. Update your application to use Postmark's SMTP server or HTTP API token in place of MessageBird's email credentials. Send a low-volume test batch first, monitor delivery and complaint rates, and then ramp traffic to 100 percent.
gotchas: Postmark does not handle SMS, WhatsApp, or voice — those need a separate provider
DNS DKIM and Return-Path changes need 24-48 hours to propagate
Postmark enforces strict separation of transactional and broadcast streams
Template variable syntax differs between providers; review every template
Inbound parsing endpoints and webhook payloads have different shapes
Pricing models differ; model real-volume cost before cutover

email.subject: Moving transactional email to Postmark
email.body:
Hi team,

Over the next 14 days we're moving our transactional email stream from MessageBird to Postmark. Email deliverability and visibility are the focus, and Postmark's transactional-only platform is a better fit than a multi-channel suite for this use case. Our SMS and other channels stay where they are.

Days 1-3: verify our sending domain in Postmark and publish DKIM records. Days 4-7: rebuild templates, import the suppression list, and wire up delivery and bounce webhooks. Days 8-11: shift a small percentage of transactional sends to Postmark and monitor inbox placement. Days 12-14: ramp to 100 percent, keep MessageBird as a fallback for one week, then decommission the email channel.

There should be no end-user impact. From addresses and email content remain unchanged. I'll share a status update at the midpoint and a final note at cutover.

Thanks,
Your customer
